responses.text: The target is a Sporting News explainer by Mike DeCourcy dated April 20, 2021, and it frames the proposed European Super League as a breakaway competition that would radically alter European soccer.

Its core description of the proposal is well supported because the official Super League release also listed 12 founding clubs, anticipated three more founding clubs, described 15 permanent founders plus five annual qualifiers, and said games would be midweek while clubs stayed in domestic leagues.
The article accurately names the six English, three Spanish, and three Italian founding clubs, matching the official announcement by the Super League organizers.

The article’s summary of the planned competition format is mostly accurate, but it omits the official detail that fourth- and fifth-place teams in each group would play a two-legged playoff for the final quarterfinal places.

The article’s financial framing is directionally accurate because the official release said founding clubs would receive €3.5 billion to support infrastructure and offset COVID impact, which is approximately the dollar figure the article reports as about $4.2 billion.

The article’s account of German clubs not joining was quickly reinforced by reporting that Borussia Dortmund, Bayern Munich, and RB Leipzig opposed the Super League and backed Champions League reforms.

One notable factual problem is the article’s match-count comparison for the Champions League, because UEFA’s April 2021 reform announcement guaranteed 10 league-stage games before knockouts while the target says the renovated plan would mean only 14 to 16 games for finalists.

The article fairly conveys the threat that breakaway participants could face international bans, since FIFA and the six confederations had previously said a closed Super League would not be recognized and that involved clubs or players would not be allowed in FIFA or confederation competitions.

The article underestimated nonlegal resistance by saying the only thing likely to stop the plan was a legal defeat, because within about 48 hours all six Premier League clubs withdrew after pressure from fans, football authorities, players, and government figures.

As a contemporaneous explainer, the piece is broadly reliable on what the Super League organizers proposed and why critics objected, but it should not be read as current status because the English clubs’ withdrawal left the original 2021 plan effectively collapsed.
